text:
TheParis 2024 Olympicsis underway and all eyes are on the French capital’s sporting venues, as they play host to the world’s best athletes. But what about the abandoned Olympic villages of years gone by?

The 2024 Games are taking place across 35 venues in Paris, which include a new Aquatics Centre constructed especially for the Olympics. This is not unusual, since many host cities build new venues for the Games.

Cast your mind back toLondon 2012, for example, and the opening of theQueen Elizabeth Olympic Park, which haslived on post-Olympicsas a green space and multi-purpose sports facility, as well as being the home ofWest Ham Unitedand the hugely popular ABBA Voyage.

But not every purpose-built Olympic venue has such impressive afterlife, with some abandoned altogether once the last medal had been won…

The 1936 Olympics in Berlin were notorious, taking place in the shadow of Adolf Hitler’s Nazi regime and its horrific ideals of racial supremacy and antisemitism.

Although Jewish athletes were either barred from taking part or sidelined to avoid offending the regime, the event saw black American track star Jesse Owens pick up four gold medals, defying the party’s race ideologies and causing consternation for the dictator, who had hoped that German athletes would dominate the games.

The onset of World War II three years later made Berlin 1936 the last Olympics until 1948.

The accommodation originally used to house athletes was then repurposed by the German army during the war, but the venues have been unused and unmaintained since then.

Most have fallen into disrepair, with mould on the ceilings and crumbling, collapsing walls.

Only the Olympic Stadium, or Olympiastadion, remains in use, now renovated and home to the football club Hertha BSC. It was also used as the venue for the recent Euros final between England and Spain.

The 1984 Winter Olympics took place in Sarajevo, the capital of Bosnia and Herzegovina, with nine new venues built for the games – several of which were constructed for the games before they had been awarded to the city.

Among the venues were the Trebević Bobsleigh and Luge Track, which was of particular note for being the fastest and steepest in the world at the time.

The track ran for 1,300 metres, with 30,000 spectators watching the bobsleigh events and 20,000 attending the luge.

However, it took on a very different role when war broke out in the country just eight years later.

The Bosnian War, which engulfed the region from 1992 until 1995, following the break-up of the former Yugoslavia, saw most of the venues from the Winter Games destroyed.

The bobsleigh track, meanwhile, became a concrete trench and artillery stronghold for Bosnian Serb forces, with defensive fighting holes drilled into the last stretch of track.

Following the war, which claimed around 100,000 lives and saw Sarajevo shelled 329 times a day on average, the track fell into disuse and later became a target for graffiti.

Restoration work on the track began in 2014, but plans for a full renovation were shelved in 2019 due to cost.

Elsewhere, a hotel built for Olympic visitors became a prison for Bosnian Muslims during the war.

On a more chilling note, a winners’ podium from the Games became a place of execution for prisoners while the conflict was ongoing.

There had been some hope that venues such as the track might be restored as part of Barcelona’s bid for the 2030 Winter Olympics, given that Sarajevo is twinned with the Spanish city.

However, since the games have now been awarded to France, the future for these disused facilities remains uncertain.

Two decades later, very little remains of the Heraklion Sports Complex, the £8bn venue which played host to the 2004 games.

The baseball and softball stadiums have been permanently shut, and are now overgrown with weeds and plants – with grass sprouting between the seats – while the kayak venue, the beach volleyball venue, and the training pools also sit unused.

The Olympic village was built on the site of what used to be an airport, and the Greek government decided not to invest further in the complex following the country’s financial crisis in 2009.

However, some of the venues have survived and are still used to day. Among them is the Ano Liosa Arena, which was converted into an arts and entertainment facility, and the OAKA area – home to the main Olympic Stadium – which has played host to concerts by the likes of Shakira and Madonna.

For the 2006 Winter Olympics in Turin, Italy, around £4.4 billion was spent on bringing the best games possible to the city, with many of the venues constructed against the breathtaking backdrops of the Alps.

18 years on, however, and little remains of the venues, which were abandoned and left to the elements and are now rusting and covered in weeds.

The village, meanwhile, was taken over by refugees and migrants from over 30 different countries including Libya and Somalia.

The Guardianreported that over 1,100 people were living in the former village in 2016 – although they were evicted by the authorities three years later.

The site has now been converted into student accommodation and social housing for up to 400 people.

Few of the venues used at the Beijing games in 2008 remained in use after the event, despite China spending an eye-watering £32bn on infrastructure.

As well as race tracks and the BMX circuit being overrun with greenery, the whitewater rafting venue has been abandoned, while the beach volleyball arena has begun to rust and rot.

Perhaps saddest of all is the demise of Olympic mascots Nini, Yingjing and Beibei, who have been left lying across the former Olympic village, surrounded by weeds.

Only the Beijing National Stadium – known as the ‘Bird’s Nest’ due to its intricate design – has survived, going on to host concerts and sporting events as well as the opening and closing ceremonies of the 2022 Winter Olympics and Winter Paralympics.

The venues built for the 2016 Olympics in Rio began falling into disrepair just six months after the games ended.

While the Arena Carioca has lived on to become part of the country’s Olympic training centre, and hosted the Pan American Gymnastics championships in 2022, others have been left to decay – despite the Games costing the Brazilian government £30bn to construct.

The famous Maracana Stadium, which hosted the opening and closing ceremonies, also suffered from vandalism and neglect, only returning to its former glory after being taken over by new management.

Perhaps the saddest story of all concerns the Aquatics Centre, which cost nearly £30 million to build but was closed by a judge amid safety concerns over stagnated water at the facility.

It was highlighted as a potential breeding ground for dengue fever as well as mosquitoes carrying the Zika virus, which struck Brazil shortly before the Games in 2016. Today, it still lies unused.

title: Eerie pictures show abandoned Olympic venues left to rot after the Games | Metro News
description: Though many cities repurpose their Olympic venues, some are left abandoned and frozen in time when the Games end, and the photos are eerie.
